speaker: interviewer
text: 6th of December, 1995. Interview of Eva Hoffman. This is tape number 2. You were saying that you had a friend--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] by the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, this-- this guy [? Gluck. ?] Did he ever inform you about what was going to happen to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think he had any knowledge neither of it. He was a youngster, too. He was even younger than I am, I think. I was altogether 16 years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did it continue that you were working for the gardeners and living in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you really why I went to work for the gardeners. They took us out to clean the ruins from the bombing. And I had like a daytime job, so I was, like, exempt from working on the-- cleaning up the-- the rubble.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daytime job was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gardening business, this what I went. We were doing rubble cleaning after the bombing. The-- supposedly the English bombed off quite a big piece of the neighborhood. So people were missing from the air raid shelter, including the second house from us, which one was somewhat bombed. 180 souls lost in that building accidentally. And we were looking for missing people. So we went to straighten out the rubble-- the bricks and masonry. There is no furniture or anything left. And help the people came, look around for their possession, to try to find. So we worked on that one. This was a compulsory labor. The Germans came and took us to do this job. And I got myself that gardening business job. So this way, I wasn't home when they came to take us every morning to go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone try and escape from the ghetto or try and commit suicide because they couldn't stand it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not after my knowledge. But like I told you, youngsters are very self-centered. They are not very interested what goes around in the neighborhood whatsoever. No-- no interest in other people, just the young ones. And [INAUDIBLE] we felt being deprived. We couldn't go here. We couldn't go there. We couldn't go to the movies. We couldn't go into an ice cream parlor for an ice cream.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Because Jews weren't allowed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, exactly. This guy [? Gluck ?] went in once, took off his Jewish star. And he haven't got the Jewish look whatsoever. He looked like an Aryan-- blonde, blue-eyed kid. And he went into the store, and he bought him-- bought two ice creams. So we were eating it under a-- a doorway. That's how we got ice cream. But this is a-- a single occurrence. No, we were extremely restricted. There was no way we could escape, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about what was going on with Jews in other areas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. My grandparents were deported. First of all, the-- my grandmother had some brothers. They were in the same town with their families, and they got deported in a different neighborhood. My grandparents had a villa, a private home. So they deported them to a different part of the city, which my grandfather wrote to me that they are living jointly with my grandmother's sisters. And I wrote to my grandmother a letter, and I got back the letter. June the 16th I had back the letter. I wrote it before my birthday, so easy to remember. I got back the letter saying that the people-- this address, the people don't live their address anymore. So it's a non-deliverable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came to know about that the-- took them away with the transport already a few days previous. So that's all what I know about whatever happened to the-- to the rest of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we heard-- my youngest uncle was married. And he was in a forced labor not far from Budapest. So he did come to visit us once or twice. He was a barber by profession. So he was like a butler to a-- a lieutenant, a general, or a captain, or whatever he was. So he did have a good position. But that didn't help him. He didn't make it neither. He died in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what-- at what point did you realize that you were not going to continue in the ghetto, that something was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a proclamation that woman of 16 till 40 years of age have to report October the 23rd on a sport place for forced labor, fully packed and with three days' food supply, which one was a joke-- who had three days' food supply?-- to report on the sport place in Budapest for-- they didn't say exactly what was the reason, but for labor department. So I ended up to be in the first transport, purely accidental.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents not want you to go? Or did they not realize what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: There really wasn't a question on not wanting to [? do ?] [? or ?] go. You see, the super of the building was a non-Jew, and she was like a watch dog. If anything would going against the grain, she would have reported us. She would be the first one to report us. All the Jewish people had to pack up under this age. I couldn't do nothing about it, that I-- I wouldn't go. That Mr.-- the young Mr. [? Gluck's ?] two sisters came with me too. But I don't know whatever happened to them. And the landlord, well, who they lived by-- she came with me, and I lost her somewhere in Belsen-Bergen. Her name was Eva, too. But she came too. And all of us, we-- who had to go, had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happen--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell-- can you describe what happened the day you left the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine a tremendous sport place that they play soccer? It's-- on the field, they were trying to make us, you know, like, per hundreds in groups. So they herd us this way and herd us that way. And they saw that was unquestionable. But finally, they corralled about 1,000 people. I was in between them. And my friend Eva and another friend of mine-- we accidentally got together just, that's all. And we started marching. They marched us out. The weather wasn't exactly to our liking neither. It start to drizzle. That's all what you need. You had a heavy bag. We took along some clean clothing, a change of clothing for two, and a little bit of food, whatever. My father came back from his military career just a few days before. And we had our knapsacks. So I had-- I gave him mine, so he gave-- he gave me his and provided me with a blanket we needed for the road. You know, like a whole military camper, all dressed up, slacks and overcoat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ended up somewhere in the outskirts of Budapest, walking, ended up in a heavy, unfinished building. So I find myself a very good spot-- cement basin that people wash. Since it wasn't finished, so I laid down in the cement basin. So I slept in there. And food-- we got a little bit of black coffee, I think. After that, we went to a different place, in a different place. Was-- was this still inside the-- the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. We were in Hungary around the surrounding villages of Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- walk there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked. And I can't give you no names on what the villages were called, but we were in quite a few. And we were digging ditches, for defense ditches. And one place we stood for about a week or 10 days. We were digging for the tanks, tank traps. I presume the Russian came and [? laughed ?] themselves silly on those tank traps what the Jewish girls digged. We had the village people. They came with us, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met a very nice young man, one of the diggers. And he went and took back some of my excess clothing to my mother. Europe clothing is a greater value than here. Like, we took along an extra pair of shoes, which one you cannot use, for, like, holidays-- whatever holiday, it never occurred-- and like a skirt and a blouse, something which one was precious in Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he brought me some money, and he brought me some jam or whatever. Twice he made the trip, and each time he says that, your mother paid me. He could have collected from me, too, but, I mean, he was decent and honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the Jewish guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a non-Jewish fellow, haven't even a name for him, one of the villagers. He was working in Budapest, and he took the job on voluntarily, that he goes to look up my family and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So he felt sorry for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was very nice. He was very nice. I had quite a bit of friends, but slowly we parted company, one reason or the other. I had a girlfriend of mine for many a years, and I had two friend of hers, and one of them got sick and-- I mean, we were not lonely like. But we done our daily job, and we got a little bit of food in the evening. And we were, like, friend like to each other. We were trying to tell each other that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Support each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: --that we'll be getting better, that we'll get better. It cannot be any worse. It will get better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, what-- what was the date of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was October the 23rd. And it lasted for probably three to four weeks, this wandering about and digging ditches around Budapest, a few days here, a few days there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was [? life for you ?] [INAUDIBLE] until the end of November.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at any time, did people try and escape? Or did you just continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a control once, took away the money if you had more than 20 Hungarian pengos. They took away the rest of the money. And a lot of people had an idea to use the money for toilet paper. So since they had outhouses, the money could be retrieved-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a lot of money. You could see the big bills in bottom of the outhouse. And they left you with the 20 pengos and no jewelry or anything. We had to give in the jewelry, if we had any previously. And they didn't really frisk you, but a little bit. You know, they wanted to feel you if you don't have anything hiding. And from there, they put us on a streetcar, and we drove to Budapest, and was considering to escape. But with my face, with black, curly hair, I don't think there would have been-- it would be a bad giveaway, like they say. And the people wouldn't-- I didn't know anybody of not the Jewish faith that would actually--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hide you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --hide me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- so then when did-- when were you given the orders that--

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we ended up-- with this traveling around, we ended up in Buda. This is the other side of the Danube.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buda, Buda, Buda-Pest.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is Buda, the other side of the Danube. It's more countryside-like. We ended up in a brick factory for the night, outdoor brick factory. We were bedding down outdoors. And guard was nice enough to us. Was pouring rain going on for quite some time. We were soaked to the skin, winter coat and all. And next day, everybody up, and here we go. No food again, account of--

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was the end of November.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the end of November. And we took the march through whole Hungary into Austria. Every night we slept on another marketplace that are selling the animals, or we slept in a hayloft. But I mean, this just wasn't us. There was quite a bit of people from Budapest. They were herding the people out of Budapest. I met my aunts-- aunt and uncle on the way. In my opinion, they was an old couple. But how my aunt tells me, they were in their early 60s. With my childish eye, they looked old people. And both of them looked like two ghosts so who couldn't walk. The old man was a barber by profession. And he had flat feet, and he couldn't walk. He was going like a duck anyway. And they shot him on the roadside. So whoever couldn't walk, they just shot them on the roadside. They didn't make no bones over it. And we were marching through whole Hungary. And this was the time when Bernadotte had the Schutz-Passes. So my father says, after all those years that I missed mine with a day. I went over the border the day before my Schutz-Pass arrived. Swedish Schutz-Pass, Switzerland Schutz-Pass.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they bringed-- brought you back, and you could, like, hold out on the consulate. This what I was told. I didn't experience it. A lot of people jumped off the bandwagon to be brought back later. People had the misconception that if they haven't got the Jewish looks-- but some of them came from the Karpaten. Their Hungarian was very weak. So this was a third giveaway. So a couple of girls tried to say they are not Jewish, and they wanted to be out. But they brought them back account of their speech difficulty. I mean, their Hungarian language wasn't clear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we passed over [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the Hungarian border with Austria. And we passed two villages, and they put it on-- put us on the train in the village of-- called Zurndorf. And we traveled about three days, back and for on the same tracks, till we ended up in Koszeg. It's 30 kilometers from the Austrian border, a small resort town in Hungary. I recognized the neighborhood from my childhood when we lived in [NON-ENGLISH]. We used to go there like for an outing. So we got unloaded by the beer factory. And we occupied the Quonset huts, which one is made out of plywood, like, and supposed to have a fireplace in the middle, which one was avoided.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were just laying around in the nighttime with all-- the whole clothing what we wore all day, account of was a bitter cold winter that year. And after a while, we went to dig ditches again, up on the hills. In Koszeg.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Koszeg, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-O-S-Z-E-G. 'Ko' means a 'stone,' a-- a nail in the stone. This what you would translate it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in December.

speaker: interviewee
text: Month of December, yes. We were digging ditches, but the groundwater came up after half a yard. So we couldn't dig too much ditches, so they got rid of some of the people. They had people living in the immediate villages. So they came to work there, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Men.

speaker: interviewer
text: --undergo selections? Or who was guarding you? What was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, who was guarding us? Like Hungarian nationals.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they cruel to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're extremely cruel. They were so vicious. That first night people were standing by the beer factory. They had one single bathroom. There wasn't, like, for ladies or for men. One single bathroom outside. And shot two people, just account of standing on line to the bathroom. Long lines. Everybody had to go sooner or later. Long lines. They were standing, and they just shot two people. One man was getting ready to expire. No medical help whatsoever. They didn't give him any medical help. The woman died right away. She was lucky. The man got some kind of a blood poisoning, I presume, account of he was around all those weeks. And he was laying in one of the smaller storage rooms indoors. And before we left from there, he was, like, saying his good-byes. Showed the picture of his wife, if he ever-- we survive, we should go and say hello to his wife. But he was a young married man, and he had small children, something. But nobody really kept any kind of mementos like that. No, they were very cruel. We worked there in the kitchen after a while, since the ground work didn't work. And since we worked in the kitchen, we were mostly indoors peeling potatoes. This was the kitchen work. I presume you would call it-- you had to have a norm how much potatoes you peeled. You just couldn't peel a little bit. You had to peel a lot. And one day, they decided there was too many of us. So they made an appell, an appeal like. They stood us up on the yard and selected the people who had any kind of a complaints. So one of my girlfriends was complaining that her feet is swollen and that she can't put on her shoes. I wore her shoe, and she had gotten a man's shoe, account of all of us had, like, frozen soles, not being able to get undressed never, account of the cold. So we were with our shoes on constantly. With our coats and with our clothes, we were wearing it for weeks. Never had a chance to take it off account of there was always cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put us in a-- by the river. They gave us a-- a barn, like, an extremely big, drafty barn. We were there for about a-- a week's time or so. Before New Year's Eve, they shipped us out to the wagons and that took us to Belsen-Bergen. What happened when they shipped you out? Did you have any kind of warning before you were going to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't tell us where we are going. Just said that we have to leave from here. But they did take [? out ?] previous transports before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people left behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In the beer factory, they had a couple of potato draggers, who were bringing in the potatoes. They remained there. And out-of-towners remained there. Afterwards, there came the out-of-towners in-- to the beer factory to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- so what happened? They told you that you were gonna be transported?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have to go somewhere else. We have to evacuate this neighborhood. I didn't know the neighborhood that well, but I know we are close to the Austrian border. So even the kitchen got evacuated, I mean, the people who cooked in the kitchen. One of our acquaintances was a cook on the kitchen, a Hungarian woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got on the-- and put us on the train again, in cattle car, and here we go again. I think four or five days they took us. Can you describe what it was like? How many people in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say it between 70 and 80.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there place--

speaker: interviewee
text: They squeezed us up.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to sit down?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had as much room that if some of them would stand, some of us could sit. But nobody died on the way. Nobody died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you food? Did they stop and open the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we stopped once. We stopped more than once. We stopped a lot of times. And we were standing, like, on a side rail. But you could see from the cattle car's window was a lot of snow, ice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once we got stopped somewhere, and they gave us horse meat, frozen. The meat was cooked. I presumed it was cooked. It was extremely salty, and it was frozen solid. So they gave us, like, a-- a piece from a slab, you know, like chopped out a little piece. So they gave us the piece of horse meat and they said the water goes with it. The piece that I [INAUDIBLE] from was frozen in. But it was so salty. There was-- actually like [INAUDIBLE]. That's how salty the meat was. It was impossible to eat it, besides being frozen and being cold enough. So that's all what we got.

speaker: interviewee
text: And another time, one of the soldiers came and let us down by the tracks to do whatever we have to do. But this was out of his own goodwill. This wasn't anything mandatory, should we say, account of the other ones they didn't let down. There was here and there a wagon, which one they opened up. We were in a sealed wagons. We got to Belsen-Bergen between three and four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't tell you that your death was going to be your destination.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't tell us nothing where we are going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got to Belsen-Bergen. It was cold, and there was snow on the ground. And we got to a big door, which one says-- says again-- Arbeits Macht-- makes you free. We walked in, and on both sides there were, like, barracks. But I wouldn't be able to tell you which part of Belsen-Bergen we came in on. We walked quite a bit. But we were so used already walking it was like an automatic thing. We were constantly walking. We walked to work six miles a day and six miles back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were working digging ditches. And when where we lived in the beer factory, we-- we walked too quite a bit of distances. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here we arrived, and they're herding us into a-- a room, a big barrack with wooden steps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody, you know, famous who was there, any camp commander who-- when [? you were-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. One I had, a woman by the name [? Ilse, ?] which one took fancy to me after-- after a while. She liked my hair very much. I don't know if that was a-- a joke by her or she really meant it. But I never got my hair cut account of [? Ilse ?] liked my hair. She always said she going to make a wig out of it. Sheitel-- she wanted a sheitel out of it. So I don't know if she had meant it serious or it was just a joke by her. But it saved my hair if-- it's not anything [? but ?] it doesn't grow back. And I was with one girlfriend in this time already. The rest of them-- no, not now, a little bit later. I still had all the girlfriends with us. And herd us in in a barrack and to take off our clothes. But it was cold enough with the clothes on. But they took away all the clothes.
